ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for egg curry; FOODON_03302184 (egg dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for egg curry as a prepared dish; A00LC (Eggs and egg products) is the closest defensible parent. A more specific prepared-dish code is not available in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for egg curry as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of egg curry consists of two hard-boiled eggs simmered in curry sauce, weighing approximately 250 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Egg curry, home-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate for egg curry
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for Turkish-style egg curry as a restaurant dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for hard-boiled eggs (NDB 01129) and a tomato-onion curry sauce base, weighted at approximately 55% egg and 45% sauce by mass. This is a reasonable compositional estimate for a standard egg curry preparation. ICMR-NIN (India) egg curry data (approximately 130 kcal/100 g) was used as a cross-reference for plausibility. The Turkish restaurant version at Sultan Palace is likely similar in macronutrient profile to a standard egg curry given the shared core ingredients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg-based and curry dishes; analogous estimate from egg and mixed vegetable curry data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Turkish egg curry. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy with egg-based curry dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Eggs have a GI of approximately 0 (negligible glycemic response); the curry sauce base (tomato, onion, spices) contributes modest carbohydrate. The blended GI of approximately 35 (low category) is consistent with similar egg curry preparations. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 4.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.2 g, rounded to 5.8 g accounting for sauce carbohydrate contribution. GL is low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 320 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 800 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size carefully, as a single serving of egg curry can contribute approximately 35% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-salt preparation or limiting sauce intake is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) and moderate carbohydrate content (4.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Egg curry has a low estimated glycemic index and low glycemic load, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content approximately 210 mg per 100 g from egg yolks; saturated fat 2.1 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Egg curry provides moderate dietary cholesterol from egg yolks; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to one serving per occasion and choose preparations using minimal oil to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg curry is a protein-rich dish consisting of hard-boiled eggs simmered in a spiced sauce typically made from onions, tomatoes, garlic, ginger, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli. The dish is widely prepared across South Asian, Middle Eastern, and Turkish culinary traditions, with each region imparting its own spice profile and sauce consistency. In the Turkish context, as served at Sultan Palace in Singapore, the preparation tends to feature a mildly spiced tomato-based gravy with Mediterranean herb influences. The texture of the dish is soft and yielding, with the egg whites absorbing the flavour of the surrounding sauce while the yolks remain firm and rich. Nutritionally, egg curry is a good source of complete protein (approximately 8.5 g per 100 g), providing all essential amino acids. It contains moderate fat (8.2 g per 100 g), primarily from egg yolks and cooking oil, with a relatively low saturated fat content. The carbohydrate content is low (4.8 g per 100 g), giving the dish a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35, making it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is a meaningful source of vitamin B12, riboflavin, vitamin D, iron, and choline from the eggs. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on preparation, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. Egg curry is commonly served as a main course alongside rice, flatbread, or salad.

confidence_reason: No exact USDA, HPB, or ICMR-NIN entry exists for Turkish-style egg curry as a restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy hard-boiled egg data and a standard curry sauce composition, cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN egg curry data for plausibility. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the core ingredient (hard-boiled egg) is well-characterised in approved sources and the sauce contribution is compositionally predictable.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (hard-boiled egg, NDB 01129) combined with a standard tomato-onion curry sauce base, weighted approximately 55% egg and 45% sauce by mass. ICMR-NIN egg curry data (~130 kcal/100 g) was used as a cross-reference. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — eggs and vegetable-oil-based curry sauce contain negligible trans fat per USDA data). GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database egg-based curry category; no direct Turkish egg curry GI measurement is available in the literature. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated at 3.2 g from USDA egg yolk fatty acid profile). All micronutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy egg data and are approximate. Sodium estimate of 320 mg/100 g reflects typical restaurant preparation with added salt; home-prepared versions may be lower. The food is served at Sultan Palace (Boat Quay) in Singapore at SGD 24.90. The cuisine tag is Turkish, though egg curry is a cross-cultural dish; the Turkish preparation context is noted. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient fields carry estimated positive values derived from USDA SR Legacy egg composition data.
